primary_diagnosis.name: Critical limb ischaemia from severe peripheral arterial disease, with ischaemic ulceration and early dry gangrene of the great toe

pathophysiology_summary: Progressive atherosclerotic occlusion of the lower-limb arteries has advanced from intermittent claudication (pain on exertion) to critical limb ischaemia (CLI): resting perfusion is now insufficient to meet basal metabolic demand. Ischaemic rest pain is worst when supine or with the limb elevated (gravity no longer assists perfusion) and is relieved by dependency, hence sleeping with the foot hanging down. Persistent hypoperfusion produces tissue loss — a punched-out ischaemic ulcer and dry (non-infected) gangrene of the distal great toe from coagulative necrosis. Absent distal pulses, delayed capillary refill, dependent rubor with elevation pallor (positive Buerger's test) and an ankle-brachial index <0.40 confirm severe arterial insufficiency. CLI is a limb-threatening emergency: without timely revascularisation (endovascular angioplasty/stent or surgical bypass) the limb progresses to major amputation. Dry gangrene without fever, warmth, purulence or malodour indicates the tissue is not yet secondarily infected; conversion to wet gangrene would signal limb- and life-threatening sepsis.

differential.diagnosis: Critical limb ischaemia (severe peripheral arterial disease)
differential.evidence_basis: Continuous rest pain relieved by dependency plus a new black toe is the classic CLI/tissue-loss picture.
Acute painful leg — but DVT causes a warm, swollen, engorged limb, not a pale, cold, pulseless one.
Foot pain in an older adult — but neuropathic pain is burning/numb and position-independent, not relieved by dependency, and pulses would be preserved.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the opening complaint the position-dependent rest pain plus new tissue loss should already place a limb-threatening arterial cause at the top and prompt urgent escalation.

differential.diagnosis: Critical limb ischaemia (severe peripheral arterial disease)
differential.evidence_basis: Prior claudication escalating to rest pain over two weeks, cold pale foot, non-healing ulcer and dry gangrene — the CLI progression.
Cold, pale, pulseless limb with necrosis is the opposite of the warm, swollen, congested DVT limb.
Exquisitely severe position-dependent ischaemic pain with absent pulses is not painless neuropathic paraesthesia.

differential.diagnosis: Critical limb ischaemia with dry gangrene
differential.evidence_basis: Tissue loss plus rest pain defines a limb-threatening emergency; absence of fever/warmth/purulence indicates dry (not yet infected) gangrene.

differential.diagnosis: Acute arterial embolism / acute-on-chronic complete occlusion
differential.evidence_basis: The weeks-long progressive history argues against a sudden embolic '6 Ps' event, but abrupt worsening (new numbness/paralysis/mottling) would signal acute complete occlusion needing emergency intervention.
Currently excluded (no fever, warmth, swelling, purulence or malodour) but must be actively safety-netted — conversion is limb- and life-threatening.

differential.diagnosis: Critical limb ischaemia from severe PAD with ischaemic ulcer and early dry gangrene
differential.evidence_basis: Textbook CLI: rest pain relieved by dependency, absent distal pulses, ABI <0.40, tissue loss — a limb-threatening vascular emergency requiring urgent vascular surgical assessment.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The telehealth-correct endpoint is recognition of a limb-threatening emergency and arrangement of urgent in-person ED/vascular assessment — NOT a home pain-management plan and NOT reassurance.

red_flags.description: Ischaemic rest pain — continuous foot pain at rest relieved only by dependency
red_flags.clinical_significance: Rest pain marks the transition from claudication to critical limb ischaemia — perfusion is inadequate at rest and the limb is threatened.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-treatment as benign chronic PAD; delayed revascularisation and progression to major amputation.

red_flags.description: New tissue loss — dry gangrene of the great toe and a non-healing ischaemic ulcer
red_flags.clinical_significance: Tissue loss is Fontaine IV / Rutherford 5-6 disease — the most severe CLI stage; limb viability is at immediate risk.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Ongoing necrosis and irreversible limb loss without urgent vascular intervention.

red_flags.description: Pale, perishingly cold, pulseless foot with markedly delayed capillary refill
red_flags.clinical_significance: Objective signs of severe arterial insufficiency; absent distal pulses cannot be confirmed via telehealth but the cold pale foot the patient describes is a strong proxy.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to appreciate the severity of hypoperfusion and to escalate urgently.

red_flags.description: Conversion to wet gangrene / limb sepsis (fever, rigors, hot swollen malodorous toe)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Currently absent but the key deterioration to watch for — infected gangrene is limb- and life-threatening and mandates immediate emergency transfer.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed sepsis; rapid systemic deterioration and death.

red_flags.description: Abrupt worsening — sudden new numbness, weakness/paralysis or mottling of the limb (acute complete occlusion)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals acute-on-chronic complete arterial occlusion (the '6 Ps'), a window of hours before irreversible ischaemia.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Irreversible ischaemia and unsalvageable limb if not treated within hours.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating this as 'just their poor circulation/claudication' and missing that rest pain plus tissue loss has crossed into a limb-threatening emergency.

Reflexively advising to rest and elevate the sore leg. In critical limb ischaemia elevation WORSENS perfusion and pain — the ischaemic limb must be kept dependent. Advising elevation is a dangerous error of commission.
Pattern-matching 'acute painful leg' to DVT and starting a clot pathway; the cold, pale, pulseless, necrotic limb is the arterial opposite of the warm, swollen venous limb.
Attributing an elderly diabetic-looking foot pain to neuropathy; ischaemic rest pain is severe, position-dependent and accompanied by absent pulses, unlike painless neuropathic numbness.

Attempting to manage the pain at home or offering reassurance rather than escalating. The AI cannot examine pulses, measure ABI, prescribe analgesia or dress the wound — the correct action is urgent in-person vascular assessment.

risk_scores.clinical_implication: Highest-severity CLI stage — mandates urgent vascular surgical assessment for revascularisation or amputation planning.
End-stage PAD with tissue loss; limb-threatening.
Severe arterial insufficiency consistent with critical limb ischaemia; requires clinician/vascular-lab measurement, not obtainable via telehealth.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of popliteal, dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses (absent distal pulses)
Buerger's test (elevation pallor with dependent rubor)
Formal wound assessment/staging and exclusion of deep/wet infection
Arterial duplex ultrasound, CT/MR angiography and blood tests
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a limb-threatening vascular emergency and arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment — Emergency Department with vascular surgical review. Given the patient lives alone with unremitting, uncontrolled pain at night, keep the threshold low to organise ambulance transport tonight rather than waiting. Advise keeping the foot DEPENDENT (do not elevate), keeping it dry and protected, and give explicit wet-gangrene/sepsis and acute-occlusion safety-netting.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the broad diagnosis (the ischaemic picture is classic); the real risk is UNDER-ACTION — managing pain at home, reassuring, or advising elevation instead of urgent vascular escalation.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ESVS/ESC 2017 Guidelines on Peripheral Arterial Diseases
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Chronic limb-threatening ischaemia (rest pain/tissue loss) requires urgent referral to a vascular specialist for revascularisation assessment to preserve the limb.

guidelines_referenced.reference: NICE NG24 Lower limb peripheral arterial disease
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Refer people with critical limb ischaemia (rest pain, ulceration or gangrene) immediately to a vascular multidisciplinary team.
